title: Chana masala
subtitle: Chickpea curry with tomato, ginger, and dried mango.
excerpt: Tinned chickpeas simmered in a deep onion-tomato base, sharpened with amchoor and finished with fresh coriander. The Punjabi tea-shop dish that became a curry-house staple. Vegetarian, cheap, ready in 35 minutes.
sourceNotes: The Punjabi chole-chana tradition with amchoor as the sharpening agent. Tea-shop versions across North India are documented through twentieth-century food writing.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well for up to 3 months. Defrost overnight, reheat gently with a splash of water.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Improves with a few hours of rest. Better the next day.

glossaryTerms.definition: Dried green-mango powder used across North India to bring acidity and a tart edge to dishes without adding liquid. Tamarind, lemon juice, or anardana (dried pomegranate) all work as substitutes, with slightly different flavour notes.

content.text: Chana masala has the rhythm of a one-pot dinner: onion fried hard, garlic and ginger in, tomato in, spice in, chickpeas in, water just to cover, simmer for 20 minutes. Amchoor (dried green mango powder) goes in at the end to lift the dish with a sour note. If amchoor isn't to hand, half a lemon's juice does the same job a little less elegantly.
Two tins of chickpeas feed four with rice. The dish improves overnight; double the batch if the fridge has room.

Heat {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in the saucepan over medium heat. Add {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds. When they crackle, after 10 seconds, tip in {{onion}} of chopped onion. Cook 10 minutes until soft and starting to brown. Stir in {{garlic}} of garlic, {{ginger-root}} of ginger, and the chopped green chilli for 1 minute.

Add {{coriander-ground}} of coriander, {{cumin-ground}} of cumin, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, {{paprika-sweet}} of paprika, and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Stir for 30 seconds. Tip in {{tinned-tomatoes}} of tinned tomatoes, breaking them up with a wooden spoon. Cook 8 minutes over medium heat, until the mixture darkens and the oil starts to separate at the edges.

Tip in {{chickpeas-tinned}} of drained chickpeas. Pour in 200 ml of water. Stir, bring to a simmer, partly cover, and cook for 15 minutes. Crush a third of the chickpeas against the side of the pan with the back of a wooden spoon to thicken the gravy.

Stir in {{garam-masala}} of garam masala and the juice of {{lemon}} of lemon (or a teaspoon of amchoor). Cook 1 minute more. Rest off the heat for 5 minutes. Scatter with {{coriander}} of chopped coriander. Serve with basmati rice, naan, or a flatbread.

items.cause: Not enough chickpeas crushed against the pan.
items.fix: Crush a generous third of the chickpeas at the simmer stage. The starch released thickens the sauce naturally.
Always finish with lemon juice or amchoor. Without the sour note, chana masala falls flat — the dish is built on that brightness.
Use fresh tins (newer than 6 months) where possible. Simmer for the full 15 minutes; older chickpeas can need 20.

content.text: Chana masala is the Punjabi tea-shop dish that became a household curry across North India and a vegetarian curry-house favourite in the UK. Eat with bhatura, the puffed deep-fried bread of Amritsar, or with steamed basmati rice if a quieter accompaniment is wanted. A wedge of raw onion with lemon juice and a sprinkle of chilli at the side gives the proper plate.
